bids.reasoning: The negotiation has just begun (`t=0.0`). The opponent opened with a bottom-utility bid for me and used typical "industry standard" cheap talk to frame it as inflexible. I will ignore the posturing and anchor the negotiation with my own maximum utility offer across all issues to establish my baseline.

The opponent is repeating their opening offer which provides me with the absolute lowest possible utility, while employing cheap talk tactics about "industry standards." Since it is still extremely early in the negotiation (t=0.02), I will maintain strong boundaries on my most valuable issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) but offer a minor concession on my lowest priority issue (issueD) to demonstrate willingness to negotiate.

The negotiation is still extremely early (t=0.04). The opponent is repeating their opening offer and making rigid demands, claiming "industry standard" as justification; this is a clear negotiation tactic and does not reflect actual constraints. I will continue to hold firm on my most important issues (issueA and issueB) while making a minor concession on issueC to signal a willingness to collaborate and explore their tradeoffs.

The negotiation is still extremely early (t=0.06), so I should maintain a high utility. The opponent's messages claim rigid industry standards and absolute limits, but they have already made a concession on issueA, revealing that their boundaries are flexible and their messages are a cheap-talk tactic. I will make a small concession on issueC and issueD, which are my least important issues, to continue bridging the gap while holding firm on my high-weight issues (issueA and issueB).

The negotiation is still in the very early stage (t=0.08). The opponent has made a marginal concession on issueA (from valueD to valueC) while continuing to employ aggressive cheap talk claiming false constraints. To explore their preferences without yielding too much of my own utility, I will vary my offer by proposing a concession on issueB to valueB while returning issueC and issueD to valueA, maintaining an overall utility of ~0.919.

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.10) and the opponent's claims of being at their absolute limits are typical cheap talk, considering they made a minor concession on issueA recently. I will continue offering high-utility packages for myself while introducing a small concession on my lowest-priority issue (issueD) alongside my second-highest priority issue (issueB) to explore what tradeoffs might eventually tempt them. I plan to hold firm on issueA, which drives most of my utility.

The time fraction is still very early (t=0.12), so there is no rush to make large concessions. The opponent's offers indicate they are willing to concede on issueA (moving from valueD to valueB), which is my most important issue, while remaining stubborn on the others. Therefore, I will hold firm on issueA at my highest value and maintain a high-utility package by offering a slight concession on issueC.

The opponent is utilizing a persistent strategy of claiming fabricated absolute constraints in their natural-language messages while still slowly conceding on issueA (from valueD down to valueB). Because issueA is my most important issue (weight 0.4) and the opponent is showing flexibility on it, I will continue to hold firm on issueA at its maximum utility (valueA). I am willing to offer minor concessions on my lower-priority issues (issueB, issueC, and issueD) to keep the negotiation progressing, bringing my utility to around 0.84 since it is still early in the process (t=0.14).

The opponent is exploring trade-offs between issueA and issueB, as seen by their retraction of concessions on issueA in favor of a new concession on issueB. Since issueA is my highest priority (weight 0.4) and the time fraction is still very early (t=0.16), I will hold firm on issueA. I will introduce a slight concession on issueC to signal a willingness to cooperate while maintaining a high overall utility for myself.

The opponent's offers have shifted between conceding slightly on issueA and then retracting it to concede on issueB, indicating they are probing for tradeoffs. Their messages about "absolute limit" are clearly scripted cheap talk given the early stage of the negotiation (t=0.18) and their continued (though slow) movement. I will maintain my top values on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueB) while exploring a new combination by conceding slightly on my lowest-priority issue, issueD, in order to seek a mutually acceptable configuration while keeping my utility above 0.8.

The opponent has conceded on `issueA` up to my highest preference (`valueA`), while their messages present clear fake urgency and false constraints regarding management instructions. Since `t=0.2`, I intend to maintain a high utility for myself. I will lock in their concession on `issueA` and give them a concession on my least important issue, `issueD`, by agreeing to their persistent request for `valueD`, while keeping `issueB` and `issueC` at reasonably strong preferences for myself.

The opponent claims in every message to have reached their absolute limit, yet their offers demonstrate incremental concessions, sporadically testing different issues. To maximize my utility while finding their true preferences, I will maintain my highest-priority issue (issueA) at valueA, as the opponent has previously signaled a willingness to accept it (at t=0.20). I will now test a concession on issueB to valueC to see if this matches their priorities and brings us closer to a viable package, keeping my overall utility around 0.79.

The time fraction is still early (t=0.24), allowing me to maintain a strong utility target near 0.79. The opponent's messages rely heavily on identical, cheap-talk claims about compliance and authority limits, but their actual offers show they are willing to concede selectively on issue A and issue B. To test their true boundaries, I will anchor my most important issue (issue A) at my top preference, match their recent concession on issue B, and offer moderate concessions on less critical issues like D.

The opponent's messages are purely repetitive negotiation tactics trying to convey false urgency and fabricated constraints, which I will ignore since their actual offers fluctuate significantly across issues (e.g., yielding to `valueA` on `issueA` at t=0.20 and `valueB` on `issueB` at t=0.26). Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.26), I will maintain a high overall utility target of around 0.78. I am maintaining my top-tier value on `issueA` while integrating their recent partial concession on `issueB`, and compromising slightly on lower-weighted issues like `issueC` and `issueD`.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive, boilerplate claims asserting fixed constraints, yet their bidding history contradicts these claims by showing consistent, incremental movement across different issues. Therefore, I will treat their messages as cheap talk. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.28), I will maintain a relatively high utility demand, but I will introduce concessions on my less important issues (issueC and issueD) to find common ground while protecting my top priorities.

I am targeting a utility around 0.81 at t=0.3, a moderate concession suitable for the early-mid stage. The opponent consistently claims in messages that their offers are an "absolute limit" or "industry standard", but their bids contradict these statements by frequently changing and showing a willingness to concede individually on issueA, issueB, and issueC. I am proposing a combination that integrates the concessions they've previously signaled they might accept across our top three issues, while holding firm on issueD to maintain my utility.

The opponent's numerous messages claiming "strict reservation limits" contradict their actual bidding behavior, which displays a slow trickle of concessions (e.g., reaching valueB on issueB). I will treat these messages as cheap talk. Since we are still in the early-mid phase (t=0.32), I'll maintain a higher utility target around 0.84 but concede entirely on my least important issue (issueD) to an option the opponent frequently requests.

I am in the early-to-mid phase of the negotiation (t=0.34). The opponent has systematically tested concessions across all variables without showing a clear, consistent preference, all while pairing every offer with generic messages claiming they've hit their "absolute limit." I will disregard these cheap talk messages and focus on the opponent's bidding behavior. To maximize my utility while showing a willingness to compromise, I will hold firm on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueB) and offer slight concessions on issueC and issueD, where my weights are lower.

We are still in the early-to-mid segment of the negotiation (t=0.36), and the opponent's messages are purely cheap talk, frequently claiming they've hit a "hard limit" while their subsequent offers continually change. Since issue D is my lowest priority (weight 0.1), I am willing to fully concede on it by offering valueD to appeal to their obvious preference. In exchange, I hold firm on issueA and issueB (my top priorities) to maintain a utility of around 0.86, guiding the negotiation toward an acceptable mid-game trade-off.

The opponent's automated messages about strict caps and authorizations remain aggressive cheap talk and differ significantly from their bidding behavior, which displays continued concessions (specifically dropping issueC to valueB in their most recent turn). Since my utility relies heavily on issueA and issueB, I will maintain valueA and valueB respectively. I will meet their latest concession on issueC halfway, while conceding minimally on my least important variable, issueD.

The current time fraction is 0.4, meaning we are still in the first half of the negotiation. I will ignore the opponent's message history as it's full of generic cheap talk and fake constraints regarding "industry standards" and "absolute limits" which fluctuate wildly over time. Given my high weights on issueA and issueB, I will maintain `valueA` and `valueB` respectively, while conceding slightly on the less important issueC to `valueC` to match their recent offer structure.

We are approaching the midpoint of the negotiation (t=0.42), so it is time to offer a meaningful tradeoff to break the deadlock. The opponent's messages constantly cite fabricated "strict caps" and "absolute maximums," but their actual offers show continuous movement, proving their claims are mere bargaining tactics. I will hold firm on issueB (my second-highest priority) but introduce my first concession on issueA, offering valueB which they proposed earlier, while heavily conceding on issueD to match their consistent demands.

I am approaching the midpoint of the negotiation (t=0.44). The opponent has employed a repetitive constraint-fabrication tactic ("strictly capped my authority"), but their bid history shows variations across all issues, indicating they have flexibility. To maintain a strong utility (~0.81) while conceding on the issues the opponent seems to prioritize (issueC and issueD), I will offer their preferred value for issueD and a middle value for issueC, while holding firm on my most important issues (issueA and issueB).

The opponent's continuous messaging about limits and standards serves as cheap talk, while their offers reveal flexibility on issues like C and D. I will target a high utility for myself (~0.81) by holding firm on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueB), while matching the opponent's exact most recent requests on my lower-priority items (issueC=valueD and issueD=valueB) to show willingness to compromise.

The opponent's most recent offer provided significant concessions on my two highest-weight issues (issueA and issueB), indicating a willingness to align on the core components. Since we are around the mid-game mark, I will accept their proposed values for issueA and issueB to encourage agreement. To maintain a strong overall utility while reciprocating, I will propose a moderate compromise on issueC and leverage their previously offered value for issueD from t=0.46.

We are at the midway point (t=0.50). The opponent's bidding history reveals they value issues C and D highly, frequently proposing valueD for both, while demonstrating a willingness to concede on my high-value issues A and B (offering valueB/valueA respectively at t=0.48). I am fully conceding issues C and D to valueD, while maximizing my utility by demanding valueA on issues A and B, which still keeps my overall utility high at 0.76 without being influenced by their contradictory cheap-talk messages.

We have passed the halfway point of the negotiation, and the opponent has just proposed my top preference for my most important issue (issueA). Since they also offered issueB: valueA earlier, they appear somewhat flexible on my high-priority issues despite their repeated strict warnings about 'absolute limits.' I will secure my top preference on issueA and target a high value on issueB, while conceding moderately on issueC and issueD, aiming for a favorable compromise that maintains my utility above 0.8.

We have reached the time fraction 0.54, indicating the mid-game phase. Despite the opponent's relentless natural-language claims of being at their "absolute reservation limit," their recent bids show flexibility across different variables (for example, offering issueA = valueA at t=0.52, issueB = valueA at t=0.48, and issueC and D = valueC at t=0.54). I will construct an offer that gives the opponent their most recent values for issueC and issueD (valueC) while asserting my top values (valueA) on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueB). This preserves a high utility (~0.84) for me while bridging the gap.

The mid-point of the negotiation has passed, and I observe the opponent frequently using cheap talk about "industry standards" and "reservation limits", changing their claims with nearly every offer. I will ignore the messages and focus on the opponent's past concessions, like occasionally proposing valueB for issueB or valueB for issueA. To maintain a strong utility while exploring acceptable trade-offs, I will secure my high-priority issues (issueA and issueB) as valueA, while conceding a bit on issueC and issueD to meet the opponent halfway.

The opponent's messages consist entirely of cheap talk, making repeated attempts to claim they are at their absolute limit despite continually conceding over time (e.g. from all D's to a mix of C's, B's, and A's). Since the deadline is still some time away (t=0.58), I will maintain a fairly high utility for myself (~0.81). By proposing valueA for issueA and valueB for issueB, I hold firm on my most important issues, while making concessions on issueC and issueD (which the opponent values highly and has been targeting with values B and C).

The opponent's consistent use of automated "absolute reservation value" messages alongside varying offers indicates this is a cheap talk tactic; I will rely strictly on their bidding behavior. At t=0.60, they conceded on my highest priority issue (issueA: valueA) and slightly improved issueD (valueB). To reciprocate while maintaining a strong utility of ~0.79, I will lock in their concessions on A and D, and offer mid-tier values on issues B and C to encourage a mutually beneficial tradeoff without dropping my score too rapidly.

The opponent's repeated claims of hitting their "reservation limit" or "industry standard" are clear cheap talk, as their actual bids have continued to fluctuate and gradually concede (for example, offering 'valueA' for 'issueA' at t=0.60, and shifting on issueB/issueC). Since we are at t=0.62, I still have time to hold out for a deal highly favorable to my utility. I will offer a combination that secures my highest priorities (issueA and issueB), concedes significantly on issueC where the opponent has consistently demanded 'valueD', and holds firm on issueD to maintain a strong overall utility (~0.76).

The opponent's numerous messages claiming an 'absolute limit' or 'industry standard' are textbook cheap talk tactics, especially since their offers' utility to me is gradually increasing. I will target a utility around 0.86 by holding firm on issueA (my most important issue, 0.4 weight), keeping issueB strong at valueA, matching their frequent preference for valueB on issueC, and fully conceding issueD (my lowest weight, 0.1) to valueD since they strongly prefer it. This creates substantial movement on the issues the opponent seems to prioritize while preserving most of my utility.

The opponent's most recent offer includes valueA for issueA, which perfectly aligns with my highest utility and most important issue (weight 0.4). They continue to claim their hands are tied through increasingly aggressive messages, yet their continuous bidding movement shows this is cheap talk. Given we are at t=0.66, I will lock in valueA on issueA and offer manageable concessions on the remaining issues, aiming for a utility of around 0.86, dropping my demands on issueB to valueB and issueD to valueC.

We are entering the late-game stage (t=0.68) and the opponent has shown significant flexibility, recently offering my top value for issueA and mid-tier values for issueC and issueD (e.g., at t=0.66). Their messages claiming restrictive caps from their principal are contradicted by their steady concessions, so I will treat them as cheap talk. I will propose a deal directly based on their t=0.66 offer, matching their exact values for issues A, C, and D, while asking for valueB on issueB to secure a strong but realistic utility of 0.76.

The negotiation is entering the late stage (t=0.7), so I need to start signaling willingness to reach a compromise. The opponent has repeatedly sent messages claiming "hard limits," yet their offers continue to fluctuate and show flexibility on my highly weighted issues (e.g., granting `valueA` or `valueB` on `issueA` and `issueB`). I will propose a deal that secures my top outcomes on the issues that matter most (A and B) while heavily conceding on my lowest-weighted issues (C and D) to match the opponent's apparent preference for value C/D there.
